title: The book that will not be published
summary: A Slovenian historian, four years after retirement, explains in a public letter why she has decided not to publish the family memoir she has finished writing. The decision is not about quality, and not about fear; it is about who else's life the book turns out to contain.
body:
Four years ago, when I retired from the institute in Ljubljana, I told three friends and two former students that I was finally going to write the family memoir I had been threatening for a decade. The draft is now finished. It runs to 312 pages. I have read it twice. I will not be publishing it, and I owe the people who asked an explanation that is more honest than the polite version I have been giving at dinner.

What I had wanted to write was a chapter of small national history seen from the kitchen table — my mother's village in the 1950s, the cooperative she joined and then quietly left, my uncle's accident at the sawmill, the year my grandmother stopped speaking Italian even at home. I am, after all, trained to handle that scale of source. I have published two books on rural cooperatives in the period. I thought my own household would yield, if anything, less resistance than the archives.

What I had not appreciated, until the draft was assembled, is how much of the book is about people who never agreed to be in it. My grandmother, of course, is dead — but she has three nieces alive in Trieste who would recognise the kitchen scenes immediately and whose mother appears in them in a light her daughters might not, on balance, welcome. My uncle's widow is alive, and although she has always been generous with me, she did not know — until I sat down with the draft last March — that I had also reconstructed, from a letter she once showed me in confidence, what she had said to my uncle the week before he died. I had thought, while writing, that the reconstruction was a tribute. Re-read in the cool morning, it is something closer to an appropriation.

The argument for publishing anyway is one I have made in print about other authors, and it is a good argument. Lives are not private property; small history needs its kitchens; the family who object are the family who most need the book. I still believe the argument in general. What I no longer believe is that it applies to this draft in the particular hands of this writer at this moment. The people I have written about did not ask to be written about, and the consolation that the writing is loving does not, on closer inspection, belong to me to offer on their behalf.

The manuscript will go into the institute's closed archive with a 40-year embargo. My nieces, if they wish, may consult it after I am dead. That is the right place for it. Not every finished book is a publishable book, and a researcher of my generation, of all people, ought to be able to tell the difference.

grammarNotes.title: Cleft + 'What I no longer believe is…' as ethical pivot
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn xoay quanh cấu trúc cleft B2 nâng cao: 'What I had wanted to write was…', 'What I had not appreciated…is how much of the book is about…', 'What I no longer believe is that it applies to this draft…'. Lặp cleft với thì khác nhau (past perfect → present) tạo nhịp 'tôi từng tin → tôi giờ không tin'. Cách dùng nominalisation ('the reconstruction', 'the consolation', 'an appropriation') cùng cụm hedging 'on balance', 'on closer inspection' là điển hình B2 cao của bài luận đạo đức.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The piece pivots on stacked B2 clefts: 'What I had wanted to write was…', 'What I had not appreciated…is how much of the book is about…', 'What I no longer believe is that it applies to this draft…'. Repeating the cleft across tenses (past perfect → present) creates the 'I once believed → I no longer believe' rhythm. Nominalisations ('the reconstruction', 'the consolation', 'an appropriation') paired with hedges ('on balance', 'on closer inspection') are hallmarks of upper-B2 ethical argument.

translationVi:
Bốn năm trước, khi tôi nghỉ hưu từ viện ở Ljubljana, tôi đã nói với ba người bạn và hai cựu sinh viên rằng cuối cùng tôi sẽ viết cuốn hồi ký gia đình mà tôi đã 'doạ' viết suốt một thập kỷ. Bản thảo đã xong. Dài 312 trang. Tôi đã đọc nó hai lần. Tôi sẽ không xuất bản nó, và tôi nợ những người đã hỏi một lời giải thích thành thật hơn phiên bản lịch sự tôi vẫn đưa trong các bữa tối.

Điều tôi đã muốn viết là một chương lịch sử dân tộc nhỏ nhìn từ bàn bếp — ngôi làng của mẹ tôi vào thập niên 1950, hợp tác xã bà đã gia nhập rồi lặng lẽ rời đi, tai nạn của bác tôi ở xưởng cưa, năm bà ngoại tôi thôi không nói tiếng Ý ngay cả ở nhà. Dẫu sao tôi cũng được đào tạo để xử lý nguồn ở quy mô đó. Tôi đã xuất bản hai cuốn sách về hợp tác xã nông thôn thời kỳ ấy. Tôi tưởng chính gia đình mình sẽ ít kháng cự hơn so với kho lưu trữ.

Điều tôi đã không nhận ra, cho đến khi bản thảo được ráp lại, là phần lớn cuốn sách nói về những người chưa bao giờ đồng ý có mặt trong đó. Bà ngoại tôi, dĩ nhiên, đã mất — nhưng bà có ba người cháu gái còn sống ở Trieste, họ sẽ nhận ra ngay các cảnh trong bếp và mẹ họ xuất hiện trong những cảnh ấy dưới một góc nhìn mà các cô con gái, cân nhắc kỹ, có thể không hoan nghênh. Vợ bác tôi còn sống, và mặc dù bà luôn rộng lượng với tôi, bà không biết — cho đến khi tôi ngồi với bản thảo hồi tháng Ba — rằng tôi cũng đã dựng lại, từ một lá thư có lần bà cho tôi xem trong sự tin cậy, điều bà đã nói với bác tôi tuần trước khi ông ấy mất. Trong khi viết, tôi tưởng việc dựng lại ấy là một sự tri ân. Đọc lại trong buổi sáng tỉnh táo, nó gần với sự chiếm dụng hơn.

Lập luận để vẫn xuất bản là lập luận tôi từng viết trong sách báo về các tác giả khác, và đó là một lập luận tốt. Các cuộc đời không phải tài sản riêng; lịch sử nhỏ cần đến những căn bếp của nó; gia đình nào phản đối chính là gia đình cần đến cuốn sách nhất. Tôi vẫn tin lập luận đó nói chung. Cái tôi không còn tin nữa là rằng nó áp dụng cho bản thảo này, trong đôi tay cụ thể của người viết này, vào lúc này. Những người tôi đã viết về không yêu cầu được viết về, và niềm an ủi rằng việc viết là đầy yêu thương, khi xem xét kỹ hơn, không phải của tôi để trao thay họ.

Bản thảo sẽ vào kho lưu trữ đóng của viện với lệnh cấm tiếp cận 40 năm. Các cháu tôi, nếu muốn, có thể tham khảo sau khi tôi mất. Đó là chỗ đúng của nó. Không phải mỗi cuốn sách hoàn thành đều là cuốn sách đáng xuất bản, và một nhà nghiên cứu thế hệ tôi, hơn ai hết, phải đủ khả năng phân biệt được.

questions.prompt: How long ago did the writer retire from the institute?
What is the state of the memoir?
options: Finished, 312 pages, read twice, but will not be published.

questions.prompt: Why is the writer offering this explanation?
options: Because she owes friends and former students a more honest reason than the polite one she has been giving at dinner.

questions.prompt: What kind of history did she set out to write?
options: A chapter of small national history seen from the kitchen table — village life in the 1950s.

questions.prompt: What was her professional expectation about writing from her own household?
options: That it would yield, if anything, less resistance than the archives.
That it would take ten years.

questions.prompt: What did she 'not appreciate' until the draft was assembled?
options: How much of the book is about people who never agreed to be in it.
How long the book would be.

questions.prompt: Who are the three nieces in Trieste?
options: Living nieces of the writer's grandmother, who would recognise the kitchen scenes and whose mother appears in them.

questions.prompt: What did she reconstruct from the letter shown to her in confidence?
options: What her uncle's widow had said to her uncle the week before he died.
What her grandmother had cooked in the 1950s.
What her mother thought of the cooperative.

questions.prompt: How does she now describe that reconstruction, on re-reading?
options: As something closer to an appropriation than a tribute.

questions.prompt: What is the 'good argument' for publishing anyway?
options: Lives are not private property; small history needs its kitchens; the family who object are those who most need the book.
That the general argument applies to this draft, in the particular hands of this writer, at this moment.

questions.prompt: Why does she say the consolation 'does not belong to her to offer'?
options: Because even loving writing is not hers to give as comfort on behalf of people who didn't ask to be written about.
Because she does not believe in love.
Into the institute's closed archive with a 40-year embargo, available to her nieces after she dies.

questions.prompt: Which is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That the manuscript is bad and should be destroyed.
That family lives appear in it without consent.
That she still accepts the general argument for publishing family material.
questions.explanation: She values the manuscript enough to archive it, and still accepts the general argument.
questions.hint: She archives the book, not destroys it.

options: Reflective, self-correcting, and ethically careful — without self-flagellation.
questions.explanation: She holds her old view in general while withdrawing it for her case.
questions.hint: She separates the general from the particular.

options: A finished book is not automatically a publishable book; respect for unconsenting subjects can outweigh a good general argument for publishing.
Family history is impossible to write.

cloze.template:
Four years ago, when I retired from the institute in Ljubljana, I told three friends and two former students that I was finally going to write the family {{b1}} I had been threatening for a decade. The draft is now finished. It runs to 312 pages. I have read it twice. I will not be publishing it, and I owe the people who asked an explanation that is more honest than the polite version I have been giving at {{b2}}.

What I had wanted to write was a chapter of small national history seen from the kitchen {{b3}} — my mother's village in the 1950s, the {{b4}} she joined and then quietly left, my uncle's accident at the {{b5}}, the year my grandmother stopped speaking {{b6}} even at home. I thought my own household would yield, if anything, less {{b7}} than the archives.

What I had not appreciated, until the draft was assembled, is how much of the book is about people who never {{b8}} to be in it. My grandmother is dead — but she has three nieces alive in {{b9}} who would recognise the kitchen scenes. My uncle's widow is alive, and she did not know that I had also reconstructed, from a {{b10}} she once showed me in confidence, what she had said to my uncle the week before he died. I had thought, while writing, that the reconstruction was a {{b11}}. Re-read in the cool morning, it is something closer to an {{b12}}.

The argument for publishing anyway is one I have made in print about other authors. Lives are not private property; small history needs its kitchens. I still believe the argument in {{b13}}. What I no longer believe is that it applies to this draft in the particular hands of this writer at this moment.

The manuscript will go into the institute's closed archive with a 40-year {{b14}}. My nieces, if they wish, may consult it after I am dead. Not every finished book is a {{b15}} book.

blanks.explanation: Never agreed to be in it.
From a letter she once showed me.
I still believe the argument in general.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The historian distinguishes between a general argument she still holds (small history needs its kitchens) and its particular application (her own draft, now, by her hand). When have you held the right principle in general but decided not to apply it in a specific case — and how did you tell the difference between conviction and self-deception?
